slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Regression? Regression vs. Classification?
slides.content:
A set of processes for estimating the relationships between a dependent
variable (a real-valued output) and one or more independent variables
(real-valued inputs).
What is the difference between regression and classification?
Regression: used to determine continuous values (e.g., price, income, temperature).
Classification algorithms: used to forecast or classify the distinct values (e.g., Real
or False, Male or Female, Cold or Hot).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Define Linear Regression
slides.content:
In short, predicting the dependent variable 𝑦 ∈ ℝ given a vector
𝐷
of independent variables 𝑥 ∈ ℝ .
Linear regression: the expected value of the output assumed
𝑇
to be a linear function of the input: 𝔼 𝑦|𝑥 = 𝒘 𝑥.
Mathematically, it is of the form:
𝑇 2
𝑝 𝑦 𝑥, 𝜃 = 𝒩 𝑦|𝑤 + 𝒘 𝑥, 𝜎
0
2
The parameters of the model are 𝜃 = (𝑤 , 𝒘, 𝜎 ).
0
Vector of params 𝒘 : the weights or regression coefficients; 𝑤 : the
1:𝐷 0
offset or bias term.
For simplicity, we can assume 𝑥 = [1, 𝑥 , … , 𝑥 ] to absorb offset term 𝑤
1 𝐷 0
into the weight vector 𝒘.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.title: Linear Regression: More Terminologies
slides.content:
If the input is a 1-D input (𝐷 = 1), the model has the form 𝑓 𝑥; 𝒘 = 𝑎𝑥 + 𝑏,
𝑏 = 𝑤 as the intercept and 𝑎 = 𝑤 as the slope – simple linear regression.
0 1
𝐷
If input 𝑥 ∈ ℝ is multi-dimensional (𝐷 > 1) – multiple linear regression.
𝐽
Further, if the output 𝑦 ∈ ℝ is also multi-dimensional (𝐽 > 1) – multivariate
linear regression. The model satisfies the fact that:
𝐽
𝑇 2
𝑝 𝑦 𝑥, 𝒘 = ෑ 𝒩 𝑦 |𝑤 𝑥, 𝜎
𝑗 𝑗 𝑗
𝑗=1

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Linear Regression for General Cases
slides.content:
For linear regression: assume linear correlation between input
and output – is this possible in real-world cases?
Unfortunately, in general, a straight line will not provide a good
fit to most datasets!
How to leverage linear regression then?
Apply a nonlinear transformation to the input features:
𝑇 2
𝑝 𝑦|𝑥, 𝜃 = 𝒩(𝑦|𝒘 𝜙 𝑥 , 𝜎 )
As long as the parameters of the feature extractor 𝜙 are fixed,
the model remains linear in the parameters.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Logistic Regression and Linear Regression?
slides.content:
Another “type” of regression that we may encounter in literature
is the logistic regression. What is logistic regression?
Logistic regression is generally NOT used for regression task!!
Instead, it is more related to the Classification task.
The output of logistic regression is usually a class label 𝑦 ∈ {1, … , 𝐶}.
Then why is it called “Logistic Regression”?
A related question: can linear regression be used for classification?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Logistic Regression
slides.content:
Yes, but not effective:
Classification – prediction of the probability above or below a threshold.
Problem with range: linear regression yield predictions below 0 or above 1, does not
align with the probability interpretation.
Problem with threshold: linear regression predictions may not easily map to a clear
classification threshold (e.g., 0.5), and this threshold can shift when new data points
are added, causing instability in predictions.
Logistic regression is designed
for classification building on
features obtained from linear
regression!
How is it achieved?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

The two key components of logistic regression:
Sigmoid (logistic) function: logistic regression incorporates the
logistic / sigmoid function that squeeze output into range [0,1].
Linear regression: logistic regression builds upon linear
regression, feeding its output to the logistic / sigmoid function.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Fitting Linear Regression Models
slides.content:
How can we fit the linear regression model to given data?
Recall what we did for the linear classifiers?
Minimize the cost function that is formulated as the mean square error
(MSE) or the least squares error (LSE).
2
𝑁 𝑇
E.g., for LSE, we want to minimize: 𝐽 𝒘 = σ 𝑦 − 𝑥 𝒘 .
𝑖 𝑖 𝑖
Can we apply similar techniques for fitting the linear regression
models to given data?
Yes, the only difference may be the choice of error (cost function).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: LSE Estimation for Linear Regression
slides.content:
The overall formulation of LSE estimation for the optimal weight 𝑤 in
linear regression is exactly the same as that for linear classifier!
The difference: elements in 𝑌 (i.e., 𝑦 , … , 𝑦 ) are now continuous values
1 𝑛
instead of discrete class label values.
𝑇 −1 𝑇
The solution 𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑋 𝑋 𝑌 is defined as the ordinary least squares
(OLS) solution. Is this solution unique?
To check if solution is unique, show that the Hessian is positive definite:
2
𝜕
𝑇
𝐻 𝑤 = LSE 𝑤 = 2𝑋 𝑋, which is positive definite if 𝑋 is full rank (i.e.,
𝜕𝑤2
the columns of 𝑋 are linearly independent).

slides.title: Geometric Interpretation of LSE
slides.content:
Safely assume that 𝑁 ≫ 𝐷 (or at least 𝑁 > 𝐷), thus there are more
observations than unknowns – an over-determined system.
෠ 𝑁
Our goal: seek a vector 𝑌 ∈ ℝ that lies in the linear subspace spanned
෠
by 𝑋 and is as close to ground-truth 𝑌: arg min ∥ 𝑌 − 𝑌 ∥ .
2
𝑌෠∈span 𝑋
෠
Since 𝑌 ∈ span 𝑋 , there would exists some weight vector 𝑤 such that:
෠
𝑌 = 𝑤 𝑥 + ⋯ + 𝑤 𝑥 = 𝑋𝑤.
1 :,1 𝐷 :,𝐷
෠
To minimize the norm of the residual 𝑌 − 𝑌, we want the residual vector to
be orthogonal to every column of 𝑋, thus:
𝑥 𝑇 𝑌 − 𝑌 ෠ = 0 ⇒ 𝑋 𝑇 𝑌 − 𝑋𝑤 = 0 ⇒ 𝑤 = 𝑋 𝑇 𝑋 −1 𝑋 𝑇 𝑌.
:,𝑑

How to interpret this geometrically?

෠ 𝑇 −1 𝑇

This corresponds to an orthogonal projection of 𝑌 onto the column
space of 𝑋.
Suppose 𝐷 = 3 equations and 𝑁 = 2
unknowns for the system 𝑌 = 𝑋𝑤. 𝑎 and 𝑎
1 2
are the columns of 𝑋, defines a 2-d linear
3
subspace embedded in ℝ .
3

orthogonal projection onto the linear
෠ ෠
subspace is denoted 𝑏. The line from 𝑏 to 𝑏
is the vector of residual errors, whose norm

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Measuring Goodness of Fit
slides.content:
How to determine if the LR model obtain really fits the data?
I.e., how to assess the goodness of fit?
For the simplest case, let us assume 1-d input, and 1-d output:
One intuitive method – Residual plots:
Residual plots: plotting the residuals 𝑟 = 𝑦 − 𝑦ො vs. the input 𝑥 .
𝑛 𝑛 𝑛 𝑛
What should we observe if the fit is good?
2
The model assumes that the residuals have a 𝒩(0, 𝜎 ) distribution, so
the residual plot should be a cloud of points more or less equally above
and below the horizontal line at 0, without any obvious trends.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Goodness of Fit: Visualize the Residual Plot
slides.content: © Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Measuring Goodness of Fit
slides.content:
For a multi-dimensional input, we can plot 𝑦ො vs. 𝑦 instead of
𝑛 𝑛
𝑟 vs. 𝑥 . A good model will have points lie on a diagonal line.
𝑛 𝑛
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

The residual plot is considered a relatively qualitative analysis.
How can we assess the goodness quantitatively – and definitively?
Computing the least squares error value (also known as the Residual
Sum of Squares RSS value) can asses the fit quantitatively.
𝑁
2
𝑇
LSE 𝒘 = RSS 𝒘 = ෍ 𝑦 − 𝑥 𝒘
𝑖 𝑖
𝑖
Another measure often used is the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):
1
RMSE 𝑤 ≜ RSS(𝑤)
𝑁
Both quantities: the smaller the better!
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Further, a more interpretable measure can be computed using the
coefficient of determination, formulated as:
σ 𝑁 𝑦ො − 𝑦 2 RSS
𝑛=1 𝑛 𝑛
2
𝑅 ≜ 1 − = 1 −
σ𝑁 𝑦ത − 𝑦 2 TSS
𝑖 𝑛
1
𝑁
𝑦ത = σ 𝑦 is the empirical mean of the responses, RSS the residual
𝑛=1 𝑛
𝑁
sum of squares, TSS is the total sum of squares TSS = σ 𝑁 𝑦 − 𝑦ത 2 .
𝑛=1 𝑛
2
𝑅 measures the variance in the predictions relative to the simple
constant prediction of 𝑦ො = 𝑦ത.
𝑛
If a model does no better at predicting than using the mean of the output, 𝑅 2 = 0.
If the model perfectly fits the data, then the RSS will be 0, so 𝑅 2 = 1.
In general, larger values imply greater reduction in variance (better fit).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Limitations of LSE and Ridge Regression
slides.content:
Fitting the LR model with LSE provides an optimal solution
given a set of input data.
Disadvantage of fitting through LSE?
Prone to overfitting – the solution is optimal ONLY w.r.t. the input data.
Any possible solutions?
To encounter overfitting – add in regularization.
E.g., Including a zero-mean Gaussian prior on the weights, formulated
−1
as: 𝑝 𝑤 = 𝒩 𝑤|0, 𝜆 𝐼 . Optimal weight obtained via MAP estimation.
This is called Ridge Regression.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Ridge Regression
slides.content:
In detail, the optimal weight is computed via MAP estimation:
1 1
𝑇 𝑇

𝑚𝑎𝑝
2𝜎2 2𝜏2
2
= arg min RSS 𝑤 + 𝜆 ∥ 𝑤 ∥
2
2 2
𝜆 ≜ 𝜎 /𝜏 is proportional to the strength of the prior; ∥ 𝑤 ∥ is defined
2
as the ℓ norm of the vector 𝑤 formulated as:
2
𝐷
∥ 𝑤 ∥ ≜ ෍ 𝑤 2 = 𝑤𝑇𝑤
2 𝑑
𝑑=1
Penalizing weights that become too large in magnitude. In general, this
technique is called ℓ regularization or weight decay.
𝟐

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Optimal Weight for Ridge Regression
slides.content:
The MAP estimate corresponds to minimizing the penalized
objective:
𝑇 2
𝐽 𝑤 = 𝑌 − 𝑋𝑤 𝑌 − 𝑋𝑤 + 𝜆 ∥ 𝑤 ∥
2
𝜆 is a hyperparameter that controls the strength of the regularizer.
The derivative of 𝐽 𝑤 w.r.t. 𝑤 is given by:
𝑇 𝑇
∇ 𝐽 𝑤 = 2 𝑋 𝑋𝑤 − 𝑋 𝑌 + 𝜆𝑤
𝑤
Hence the optimal weight 𝑤ෝ is thus formulated as:
𝑚𝑎𝑝
−1
𝑇 −1 𝑇 𝑇
𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑋 + 𝜆𝐼 𝑋 𝑌 = ෍ 𝑥 𝑥 + 𝜆𝐼 ෍ 𝑦 𝑥

slides.content:

Assume 𝑁 > 𝐷, i.e., we have more samples than the number of
features per sample – denoted as a over-determined system.
Question: what if 𝑁 < 𝐷 , i.e., we DON’T have enough samples?
There will be more than one unique solution!
𝑇
We then need to constrain the search. Assume (𝑋𝑋 ) is invertible,
𝑇 𝑇
denote 𝑤 = 𝑋 𝑎, then we have 𝑋𝑋 𝑎 = 𝑦 from 𝑋𝑤 = 𝑦.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Ridge Regression for Less Samples
slides.content:
Now, what if 𝑁 < 𝐷, which is often the case where we would
require extra regularizations for regression.
𝑇 𝑇 −1
Note that without regularization, we have 𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑋𝑋 𝑦.
For 𝑁 < 𝐷, the optimal weight is formulated as:
𝑇 𝑇 −1
𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑋𝑋 + 𝜆𝐼 𝑌
𝑚𝑎𝑝 𝑁
This is referred to as the dual form of the ridge regression result, where
𝑇 −1 𝑇
the form of 𝑤ෝ = 𝑋 𝑋 + 𝜆𝐼 𝑋 𝑌 is regarded as the primal form.
𝑚𝑎𝑝 𝐷
Leave a question, how to obtain the 𝑤ෝ in its dual form?

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf

slides.content:

slides.title: A More Extreme Regularization
slides.content:
Recap: for ridge regression, we assumed a Gaussian prior for the
−1
regression coefficients when fitting the LR models: 𝑝 𝑤 = 𝒩 𝑤|0, 𝜆 𝐼 .
𝐷
Why is it a good choice?
Encourage small parameters – prevents overfitting.
Is this enough for ALL cases – obviously NOT.
Sometimes we want the parameters to not just be small, but to be exactly
zero, i.e., we want 𝑤ෝ to be sparse.
Why sparse representation? (Or sparse model?) – think first from model point of view:
Leads to simpler model by learning what parameters can be dropped, lowering their
total number.
Produce models that are smaller in terms of memory usage.
How to achieve such a goal?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Goal: parameters that are exactly zero: minimize the 𝐿0-norm.

0 𝑑
𝑑=1
From the feature point of view, sparse models are also useful
as it can be used to perform feature selection.
𝐷

𝑑=1 𝑑 𝑑
If any 𝑤 = 0, ignore the corresponding feature 𝑥 .
𝑑 𝑑
This idea can be applied to nonlinear models, such as MLP!
The next question: how to compute such sparse estimate?

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Question: How can we leverage Trees
slides.content:
A Quick Review: Trees
for Classification or Regression?
Tree data structure is a data structure to store data in hierarchical manner.
Tree data structure has roots, branches, and leaves connected.
Some basic terminologies:
Parent Node: the predecessor of a node is called the parent node of that node.
Child Node: the immediate successor of a node is called the child node of that node.
Root Node: The top-most node of a tree or the node
which does not have any parent node is the root node.
Leaf Node or External Node: The nodes which do not
have any child nodes are called leaf nodes.
Sibling: Children of the same parent node are siblings.
Level of a node: The count of edges on the
path from the root node to that node. The
root node has level 0.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Regression Trees
slides.content:
In general, we leverage the Classification and Regression Tree (CART)
for classification and regression tasks.
More familiar terminology: Decistion Tree (DT)
First consider regression trees:
Inputs are real-valued.
Tree consists of a set of nested decision rules.
How does it work in general?
At each node 𝑖, the feature dimension 𝑑 of input 𝑥 is compared to threshold 𝑡 , and
𝑖 𝑖
the input then passed down to the left or right branch depending on the comparison.
At the leaves of the tree, the model specifics the predicted output for any input that
falls into that part of the input space.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

What is the Loss?
How to Minimize the Loss?

Note: finding the optimal partitioning of the data is NP-complete. How?

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: CART: A Numerical Example
slides.content:
Consider house prices in SG. Our target is to predict the variable price 𝑃.
The attributes (input) are house size 𝑆 and number of rooms 𝑅, data as follows:
Now, how to leverage CART to predict the price of a new house given its house size 𝑠
and its number of rooms 𝑟? Suppose the CART is simple that it only contains one root
and all the other nodes are leave nodes.
I.e., we only split once for the tree.
Question: under which and what criterion should we split the root?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

First possibility: split via 𝑆. Suppose we set the threshold as 𝜏 = 0.75.
The targets of the two nodes are 0.19, 0.23 and 0.28,0.42,0.53,0.75,0.80 .
We leverage the MSE as the cost function, and we compute the cost at each node as:
1
∗ 2
𝑐 𝒟 = 𝑀𝑆𝐸 = ෍ 𝑦 − 𝑦ത
𝑖 ∗ 𝑛
𝒟
𝑖 ∗
𝑛∈𝒟
𝑖
Therefore: 𝑐 0.75 𝐿 = 4 × 10 −4, 𝑐 0.75 𝑅 = 0.0385
2 5
𝐿 𝑅
𝑐 0.75 = 𝑐 0.75 + 𝑐 0.75 = 0.0276
7 7
We can sweep through some possible thresholds and their corresponding costs are:
The best possible threshold 𝜏 for attribute 𝑆 would be 𝜏 = 2.5.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Second possibility: split via 𝑅. Similar to how we analyze the split via 𝑆,
we can obtain a set of cost function values w.r.t. different thresholds 𝜏.
We can view that the least cost function is obtained by splitting via 𝑆 (space) with a
threshold of 𝜏 = 2.5. The left branch is where 𝑆 < 2.5 and the right branch is where
𝑆 ≥ 2.5.
Therefore, if a house size is of 𝑠 = 3.3 and 𝑟 = 4, the output (price of this house) with
this regression tree is obtained by computing the average of all the house with 𝑆 ≥ 2.5:
0.53 + 0.75 + 0.80
𝑝 = = 0.6933
3
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: CART: Handling the Special Cases
slides.content:
What is the extreme case for trees?
If we let the tree become deep enough, it can achieve 0 error on the
training set by partioning the input space into sufficiently small regions.
Result in overfitting!
To prevent this, there are two main approaches.
1. Stop the tree growing process according to some heuristic, such as
having too few examples at a node, or reaching a maximum depth.
(Early stop)
2. Grow the tree to its maximum depth, where no more splits are
possible, and then to prune it back, by merging split subtrees back into
their parent. (Tree pruning)
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: The Pros and Cons of CART / DT
slides.content:
Trees are very popular, especially in industrial applications!
Why? What are the characteristic that allows them to be applied in the
industrial scenarios?
Think of what characteristics are more required in these scenarios.
Easy to interpret, thus fast to fit, and scale well to large data sets;
Easily handle mixed discrete and continuous inputs;
Insensitive to monotone transformations of the inputs, so there is no
need to standardize the data.
Ability to perform automatic variable selection.
Relatively robust to outliers.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

But, you may not see trees often in research or in SOTA:
The primary disadvantage of trees is that they do not predict
very accurately compared to other kinds of model.
In part due to the greedy nature of the tree construction.
Another side effect of greediness: trees are unstable: small changes to
the input data can have large effects on the structure of the tree.
This is also related to the hierarchical nature of the tree-growing
process, causing errors at the top to affect the rest of the tree.
Omitting even a single data point from the training set can result in a
dramatically different decision surface
An example to illustrate the unstable tree.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Unstable DT: Example with the IRIS dataset
slides.content: © Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Can We Stabilize the DT?
slides.content:
Decision trees (DTs) can be quite unstable, due to their
predictions may vary a lot if the training data is perturbed.
I.e., decision trees are a high variance estimator.
Question: how to reduce the variance? (Note that we only care about
the variance first, we do not care about the bias)
A simple way to reduce variance is to combine multiple models.
This is called ensemble learning.
The ensemble will have similar bias to the base models, but lower
variance, generally resulting in improved overall performance
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Ensemble Learning: Combining Models
slides.content:
When discuss about “combining” models:
Such discussion focus on the combination of the results of the models.
The combination for regressors and classifiers could be different,
thanks to the different type of output (continuous vs. discrete)
For regression models, a sensible combination method is averaging:
1
𝑓 𝑦 𝑥 = ෍ 𝑓 (𝑦|𝑥)
𝑚
ℳ
𝑚∈ℳ
For classifiers, it can sometimes be better to take a majority vote of the
outputs (committee method or voting method).
How does the voting works and why does voting improve accuracy?
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Ensemble Learning: Voting
slides.content:
Suppose each base model is a binary classifier with the same accuracy 𝜃.
Suppose class 1 is ALWAYS the correct answer. 𝑌 ∈ {0,1} be the prediction for the
𝑚
𝑚-th model, and let 𝑆 =
σ𝑀
𝑌 be the number of votes for class 1.
𝑚=1 𝑚
For majority voting: the final prediction would be 1 if 𝑆 > 𝑀/2. Thus the probability
that ensemble will predict class 1 is computed:
𝑀 𝑀
𝑝 = 𝑃 𝑆 > = 1 − 𝐵( , 𝑀, 𝜃)
2 2
Here 𝐵(𝑥, 𝑀, 𝜃) is the cdf of the binomial distribution with parameters 𝑀 and 𝜃
evaluated at 𝑥. For 𝜃 = 0.51 and 𝑀 = 1000, the result is 𝑝 = 0.73. By increasing 𝑀 =
10000 the result is further improved to 𝑝 = 0.97.
The performance of the voting approach is dramatically improved, because we
assumed each predictor made independent errors. In practice, their mistakes may be
correlated, but as long as we ensemble sufficiently diverse models, we can still come
out ahead.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Bagging: an Introduction
slides.content:
The original ensemble learning (or called the vanilla ensemble learning)
with averaging or voting is somehow too intuitive.
It also does not make any significant limitations and/or guidance towards
each individual model for ensembling.
A “better” form of ensemble learning: bagging (bootstrap aggregating):
Fit 𝑀 different base models to different randomly sampled versions of the data;
Encourages the different models to make diverse predictions;
The datasets are sampled with replacement (bootstrap sampling)
A given example may appear multiple times, until we have a total of 𝑁 examples per
model (𝑁 is the number of original data points).
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: The Cons and Pros of Bagging
slides.content:
Now that we know the general process of bagging – what could be the
(dis-)advantages of bagging?
The key of bagging – each model sees sampled data;
Each base model only sees on average 63% of the unique input examples! (Why?)
The chance that a single term will NOT be selected from a set of size 𝑁 in any of 𝑁
𝑁
1
draws is 1 − . With large 𝑁 → ∞, this becomes 𝑒 −1 ≈ 0.37.
𝑁
This 37% of the training instances that are NOT used by a given base
model are called out-of-bag instances (oob).
Can use the predicted performance of the base model on these oob instances as an
estimate of test set performance.
This provides a useful alternative to cross validation
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

Main advantage of bagging: prevents the ensemble from relying too much
on any individual training data, enhances robustness and generalization.
BUT, bagging does not always improve performance. In particular, it relies on the
base models being unstable estimators, so that omitting some of the data significantly
changes the resulting model fit (for trees)
Quite mixed for others, e.g., bagged DNNs do not usually work well as deep networks
will underperform if they only see 63% of the data.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Decorrelate Base Models : Random Forests
slides.content:
(more)
Bagging relies on the assumption that re-running the same
learning algorithm on different subsets of the data will result
in sufficiently diverse base models.
The key is: diverse base models. In other words, the models should not
correlate with each other.
Another technique that further decorrelate the base learners is
known as random forests.
Learning trees based on a randomly chosen subset of input variables
(at each node of the tree), as well as a randomly chosen subset of
data cases.
© Copyright National University of Singapore. All Rights Reserved.

slides.file: Lecture 4 Linear regression and Nonparametric methods.pdf
slides.title: Random Forests: Process and Example
slides.content:
The general process: modify the tree splitting equation so that the feature
split dimension 𝑗 is optimized over a random subset of the features.
A simple recap of the tree splitting equation:
𝐿 𝑅
𝒟 𝑗, 𝑡 𝒟 𝑗, 𝑡
𝑖 𝐿 𝑖 𝑅
𝑗 , 𝑡 = arg min min 𝑐 𝒟 𝑗, 𝑡 + 𝑐 𝒟 𝑗, 𝑡
𝑖 𝑖 𝑖 𝑖
𝒟 𝒟
𝑗∈{1,…,𝐷} 𝑡∈𝒯
𝑗 𝑖 𝑖
E.g., for a email spam dataset, there are different types of features (57):
48 features corresponding to the percentage of words in the email that match a given
word, such as “remove” or “labs”;
6 features corresponding to the percentage of characters in the email that match a
given character;
3 features corresponding to the average length, max length, and sum of lengths of
uninterrupted sequences of capital letters.

slides.title: Visualize the Performance of RF vs. Bagging
slides.content:
RFs work much better than bagged decision trees, because
many input features are irrelevant.
